@@ -149,3 +149,14 @@ npm run build
 ```
 
 Capture a manual smoke log (`start`, `send`, `peek`, `log`, `watch`) to document real-world behavior.
+
+## Appendix: Detached Execution Internals
+
+`start` and `send` launch Codex in a **separate Node process** on purpose:
+
+- The primary Codex session needs to return immediately. Forking another process prevents the parent CLI from blocking on the long-running `codex exec` invocation.
+- Each detached helper gets a clean environment (its own PID tree, sandbox policy, cwd, etc.). If the helper crashes, the parent stays healthy and `peek` simply shows that the thread stopped.
+
+To keep everything in a single distributable file, the CLI now re-invokes itself with hidden subcommands (`worker-start` / `worker-send`). Those commands aren’t listed in `--help` because they only accept a base64 payload generated by the launcher. You never call them directly—when you run `codex-subagent start`, the CLI serializes the prompt/metadata, spawns itself with `worker-start --payload <base64>`, and that inner process runs `runStartThreadWorkflow`. The same pattern applies to detached `send`.
+
+This architecture is invisible to day-to-day operators, but it’s worth knowing when debugging: if you see a background process named `codex-subagent worker-start`, that’s the detached helper re-entering the CLI.
